mappings.rationale: Directly addresses the nature, scope, and methods of the social sciences, the central subject of this section.
Section B covers the nature of anthropology including its background, scope, and division between cultural and physical anthropology.
Section C covers the nature of sociology including background, methodology, status, and emergent trends.
Section D covers the nature of economics including theories, scope, methods, and cognate disciplines.
Section D.2 lists microeconomics as part of the scope and methods of the study of economics.
Behavioural economics incorporates psychological insights into economic theory, reflecting the changing character of disciplines (A.3.d).
Section E covers the nature of political science including its history, scope, methods, and cognate disciplines.
Political philosophy connects to the nature of political science (E) and the influence of political theories on social sciences.

mappings.vsiAuthor: John A. Matthews, David T. Herbert
mappings.rationale: Section F covers the nature of geography including its history, scope, and division between physical, human, and regional geography.
Human geography is part of the scope of geography (F.2) and studies migration, inequality, and spatial relationships.
Section G covers the history and methods of psychology including its nature, scope, and special branches.
Section H covers the nature of linguistics including its history, nature, and scope.
Languages relate to linguistics (H) including the study of phonology, word order, and language diversity.
Sociolinguistics bridges sociology (C) and linguistics (H), illustrating cross-disciplinary approaches (A.3.d.i).
Psycholinguistics bridges psychology (G) and linguistics (H), illustrating the interdisciplinary nature of these fields.
Multilingualism is a topic within linguistics (H) connected to sociolinguistic and anthropological approaches.
The study of English relates to linguistics (H) and the historical development of language study.

mappings.vsiAuthor: Emma Borg and Sarah A. Fisher
mappings.rationale: Explores how language conveys meaning -- a foundational question in linguistics (H) that also connects to philosophy and psychology.
Translation studies are connected to linguistics (H), involving theoretical and practical study of language.
Section C.5 lists social psychology as a cognate discipline of sociology, and G.3 mentions it as a branch of psychology.
Section C.5 lists criminology as a cognate discipline of sociology.
Section C.5 lists demography as a cognate discipline of sociology.
Section E.3 lists international relations as a cognate discipline of political science.
Section A.3.b specifically identifies the influence of Marxism as a major 20th-century development in the social sciences.
Section A.3.c identifies the influence of Freudian ideas as a major 20th-century development in the social sciences.
Section A.3.c covers the influence of Freudian psychoanalytic ideas on 20th-century social sciences.
Section A.1.b identifies the heritage of the Enlightenment as a key origin of the social sciences.
Section A.2.b mentions social statistics, and A.3.d.iii-iv covers quantitative methods and empiricism in social sciences.
Illustrates the scope of psychology (G) through its study of reasoning, judgment, and decision-making -- topics that also inform economics (D) and political science (E).
Explores one of psychology's central questions -- the nature of conscious experience -- bridging physiological psychology (G.3) and philosophy of mind.
Represents developmental psychology, one of the special branches listed under G.3, covering how cognition and language emerge in childhood.
Represents clinical practice as a special branch of psychology (G.3), showing how the discipline applies its methods to mental health.
Exemplifies physiological psychology (G.3) by showing how brain imaging and neuroscience methods have transformed the study of cognition.
Traces the behaviorist and cognitive traditions that shaped psychology's methodology (G.2), from Pavlov and Skinner to modern learning theory.
Globalization is a cross-disciplinary social science topic touching economics (D), political science (E), and sociology (C).
Geopolitics connects political science (E) with geography (F), exemplifying cross-disciplinary approaches (A.3.d.i).
Democracy is a foundational concept in political science (E), and its study illustrates the scope and methods of the discipline.
Examines populism as a political phenomenon studied within political science (E), reflecting the discipline's engagement with contemporary movements.
Nationalism is a central concept studied across political science (E) and sociology (C), illustrating how identity shapes political movements and state formation.
The study of ideology is central to political science (E) and the theoretical frameworks of social sciences.
Capitalism is central to economics (D) and relates to the influence of economic theories on social sciences (A.2.a).
Feminism has been a major theoretical influence on sociology (C) and the changing character of disciplines (A.3.d).
A topic studied through sociological (C) and anthropological (B) frameworks, illustrating how the social sciences analyze structural inequality.
Identity is studied across sociology (C), anthropology (B), and psychology (G), making it a natural example of cross-disciplinary approaches (A.3.d.i).
International migration is studied across sociology, economics, and political science as a cross-disciplinary topic.
Physical anthropology, including human evolution, is part of the scope of anthropology (B.2).
Tocqueville's work on democracy and society is a foundational contribution to political science and sociology.
Malthus's work on population connects demography (C.5), economics (D), and social science history.
Adam Smith is foundational to the development of economics (D) and the emergence of social science.
Keynes is pivotal in the development of economic theory (D) and macroeconomics (D.2).
